sanskrit: दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत् । स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत् ॥
hindiTranslation: जो कुछ कर्म है, वह दुःखरूप ही है  ऐसा समझकर कोई शारीरिक क्लेशके भयसे उसका त्याग कर दे, तो वह राजस त्याग करके भी त्यागके फलको नहीं पाता ॥
englishTranslation: If someone gives up action thinking it is only suffering and fearing bodily strain, that renunciation is passionate, and it brings no fruit of renunciation.
essence: Fearful withdrawal is not freedom; it is avoidance wearing a noble name.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Giving up your responsibilities because they feel hard is not true renunciation. It is a restless, fear-driven rejection of effort.
simpleInsight: Real letting go is clear and strong, not scared and avoidant.
detailedMeaning: On Kurukshetra, Arjuna is still trapped between action and retreat. Krishna corrects a subtle mistake: abandoning duty because it feels painful is not purity, only fear in disguise. That kind of giving up brings no inner gain.
modernRelevance: You quit the hard conversation, then tell yourself you are “protecting your peace.” The body may feel safer, but the mind knows the move was avoidance, not freedom.
translationLiteral: दुःखम् (suffering) / इति एव (just so) / यत् (which) / कर्म (action) / कायक्लेश-भयात् (from fear of bodily strain) / त्यजेत् (would abandon) / सः (that one) / कृत्वा (having done) / राजसम् (passionate) / त्यागम् (renunciation) / न (not) / एव (indeed) / त्याग-फलम् (fruit of renunciation) / लभेत् (would obtain)
insight: It separates true renunciation from escape, so you stop dressing fear as virtue.

quoteVariants.text: Fearful refusal is not renunciation. It leaves you with nothing.
I’m not renouncing—I’m just avoiding strain.
